Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed declarative clause; possessive noun phrase + stative predicate with ya; body-part metaphor centered on ɲaha ‘face’.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone looks severe, angry, proud, or emotionally closed, so others should approach carefully.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Wu ya tɔɔyɔ ni, a ma nɔhɔpiire ɲa fɔɔri.
Usage Contexts: family mediation; market disagreement; community meetings; warnings to children
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Face-based social reading is important in courtyard and market interaction; a ‘heavy face’ signals tension before words are spoken.
First Example Sentence: Bakari ɲaha ya gbo, pee ganha jo na kaɲɛhɛ ni wɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Ma ba Fanta ɲa sisan, ma jo fɔlɔ; wu ɲaha ya gbo.
Third Example Sentence: Nɔhɔcɛrɛɛ bɛɛri di ɲa na koro wu ɲaha ya gbo caŋa ki na.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed clause; possessive noun phrase + stative quality; metonymy using ɲɔ ‘mouth/speech’.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The person speaks clearly, persuasively, and with social tact.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Wu ya jomɔ jo naŋa ni, sipya bɛɛri da wu mɛɛ.
Usage Contexts: public speaking; trade negotiation; praise; storytelling; teaching
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Elders and griot-like speakers are valued for speech that settles disputes and makes difficult matters understandable.
First Example Sentence: Awa ɲɔ ya jɛni; a di bɛɛri fanhaɲɛhɛŋɛ fɔɔ na.
Second Example Sentence: Karamɔgɔ wu ɲɔ ya jɛni, kɔrɔgɔ bɛɛri di sɛmɛ ki mɛɛ.
Third Example Sentence: Jatigi wu di jo, «Musa ɲɔ ya jɛni, a da yaha ki shɔ.»

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal idiom in a locative clause; abstract noun funŋɔ ‘inner feeling/heart’ placed inside kɔɔn ‘belly’.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person is hiding pain, resentment, fear, or a serious thought instead of saying it openly.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Wu ma wu jomɔ jo; a funŋɔ ya tɔɔyɔ ta.
Usage Contexts: private counsel; marriage talk; grief; friendship; conflict avoidance
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: In many Mande and Senufo-area speech traditions, the belly/inside is a metaphorical place for concealed emotion.
First Example Sentence: Fanta di sɔrɔ, ga funŋɔ ki ya kɔɔn ni.
Second Example Sentence: Ma ɲɔ ya yala, ga nɛ ɲa na funŋɔ ki ya kɔɔn ni.
Third Example Sentence: Caŋa kili na, koro wu bɛ di jo na: «Ma funŋɔ ki ya kɔɔn ni wɛ?»

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed clause with body-part subject and motion verb; ɲuŋɔ ‘head’ acts as seat of attention.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone is distracted, absent-minded, or mentally far from the present situation.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Wu ma mɛɛ fiin; wu ɲiga ya yaha wá.
Usage Contexts: school; work; farming coordination; joking criticism
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: A wandering head evokes someone whose attention has gone down the road before their body follows.
First Example Sentence: Sira ya sɛmɛ ki kalaa, ga wu ɲuŋɔ ya yaha shɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Musa, ma mɛɛ! Ma ɲuŋɔ ya yaha shɛ sugu funŋɔ ni.
Third Example Sentence: Koro wu di ɲɛɛri na a ɲuŋɔ ya yaha shɛ yɛrɛ caŋa bɛɛri.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Short stative clause; lɔhɔ ‘hand’ as subject with wolo ‘open’; body-part metaphor for generosity/action.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone is generous, ready to share, or willing to help without being forced.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Wu ya fenɛ shɛ ni sipya wuu ni.
Usage Contexts: hospitality; family support; praise; communal labor
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Sharing food, labor, and small resources is central to village and urban-neighborhood reciprocity.
First Example Sentence: Aminata lɔhɔ ya wolo; a di ji ni dumuni shɛ jatigi mu.
Second Example Sentence: Wu lɔhɔ ya wolo, lee k’a pyi nɔhɔ bɛɛri ni.
Third Example Sentence: Nɛ da ma tɔɔyɔ soro, bari ma lɔhɔ ya wolo caŋa bɛɛri.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Possessive/existential construction; togo ‘name/reputation’ placed upon the person with fe ‘on/at’.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The person has a visible reputation, whether honorable or notorious, that people recognize immediately.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Sipya bɛɛri ya wu tɔɔyɔ cɛ.
Usage Contexts: community reputation; politics; business; moral evaluation
Register and Tone: neutral, can be admiring or ironic
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Names carry lineage, honor, and social memory; a name ‘on’ someone means public identity has become attached to them.
First Example Sentence: Koutiala ni, togo ya Adama fe, bari a ya jomɔ shɔ naŋa ni.
Second Example Sentence: Togo ya wu fe, ga a ganha ma yɛrɛ gbɔɔn wɛ.
Third Example Sentence: Sugu funŋɔ ni, pee di jo na togo ya Fanta fe.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Personified clause; saha ‘field’ acts as teacher; experience-as-school metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Hard experience has taught someone better than advice alone could.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Wu ya cɛ, bari keree wii di wu kalaa.
Usage Contexts: agriculture; life lessons; elder advice; resilience
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Agricultural work around millet, sorghum, and cotton creates strong metaphors for patience, timing, and consequence.
First Example Sentence: Saha ya wu kalan; sisan Bakari ma funŋɔ shɔ kanhama la wɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Ma ba ji caŋa cɛ wɛ, saha da ma kalan yɛrɛ ni.
Third Example Sentence: Koro wu di jo na saha ya wu kalan, a bɛ ganha yaha kɔrɔgɔ wɛ.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Locative-body idiom; kɔɔn ‘belly/inside’ closes jomɔ ‘speech/word’; transitive/causative flavor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person keeps a secret, restrains speech, or refuses to reveal what they know.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Wu ma mɛɛ wolo; wu ya sɔrɔ mɛɛŋɛ.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Keeping words inside is valued when premature speech could shame a family or damage a negotiation.
First Example Sentence: Jatigi kɔɔn ki ya jomɔ daan fɔɔ bɛɛri ma zhɛ wɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Nɛ cɛ na ma kɔɔn ki ya jomɔ daan, ga sisan jo nɛ mu.
Third Example Sentence: Fanta ma saari jo wɛ; wu kɔɔn ki ya jomɔ daan.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Personified natural-force clause; gboo ‘dust’ carries tɔɔyɔ ‘name/matter’; metaphor of rumor spreading.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: News, rumor, or fame has spread quickly beyond its original place.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Jomɔ ki ya shɛ yaha bɛɛri ni.
Usage Contexts: gossip; market news; politics; social media analogy; reputation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Dust rising from roads, markets, and cattle paths gives a local image for news traveling fast through communities.
First Example Sentence: Gboo ya wu tɔɔyɔ ta; caŋa kili ni, bɛɛri di mɛɛ ki cɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Ma jo ŋɔmɔŋɔ ni, ga gboo ya ma tɔɔyɔ ta Koutiala ni.
Third Example Sentence: Pee ganha ŋmɛɛ wɛ, bari gboo ya tɔɔyɔ ta yaha ki na.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Short metaphorical clause; ji ‘water’ acts on ɲɔ ‘mouth’; experiential metaphor for stunned silence.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone becomes speechless, embarrassed, or unable to answer because the situation overwhelms them.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Wu ma jaabi ta wɛ, a ya yɛrɛ ɲa na.
Usage Contexts: surprise; shame; public questioning; humor; dispute settlement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Water cooling or covering the mouth evokes the social moment when words fail before elders or a crowd.
First Example Sentence: Koro wu di ɲɛɛri, ji ya Musa ɲɔ shɔ.
Second Example Sentence: Ba pu ma keree ki ɲa, ji ya pee ɲɔ shɔ.
Third Example Sentence: Awa ya mɛɛ kili jo naŋa ni, ji ya sipya wuu ɲɔ shɔ.
